messages.content:
Post colonial theories focus on
Select one:
a. Exploitation and racism
b. Eurocentric epistemologies
c. The history of European empires
d. The culture of indigenous peoples
a. Exploitation and racism

What sparked the #feesmustfall movement in South Africa?
Select one:
a. The decolonization of the educational system
b. Gender and racial inequalities
c. Unaffordable university tuitions
d. The outsourcing of general workers
b. Gender and racial inequalities

Marked out of 1Not flaggedFlag question
Question text
What is the history of Bantu education?
Select one:
a. Shift from production to service sector
b. A system of education under apartheid
c. The third industrial revolution
d. The first industrial revolution
b. A system of education under apartheid

Which statement accurately describes the post-apartheid government's view on schooling in South Africa?
Select one:
a. Schooling was the easiest institution to reshape
b. Schooling played a minor role in the transformation of apartheid
c. Schooling was symbolic of the old order and needed to change
d. Schooling required minimal effort to address inequalities
c. Schooling was symbolic of the old order and needed to change

How would you define apartheid?
Select one:
a. Racial segregation and discrimination
b. The shift from labour intensive to knowledge intensive production
c. The fusion of physical, digital and biological worlds
d. The fourth industrial revolution
a. Racial segregation and discrimination

nstitutional power relates to
Select one:
a. Shaping social, political and cultural agendas
b. Strategies employed by groups and institutions in exercising authority
c. Access to resources and control over their allocation
d. Contestations over meanings
b. Strategies employed by groups and institutions in exercising authority

According to Christie (2002), what continues to be a major predictor of social and schooling outcomes in South Africa?
Select one:
a. Poverty and unemployment
b. Educational reforms
c. Political independence
d. Racial segregation
d. Racial segregation

Which curriculum replaced Bantu Education in South Africa?
Select one:
a. Curriculum 2005
b. FET
c. Power of colonial administrations
d. CAPS
a. Curriculum 2005

According to Seroto ,Davids and Wolhuter (2020) where are educational inequalities more visibly pronounced?
Select one:
a. Developing countries
b. Developed countries
c. Global South
d. Global North
c. Global South

According to Christie (2020) what is evident in the South African schooling system?
Select one:
a. Racial geography of apartheid
b. Effective teaching practices
c. Integration of African languages
d. Equal resource distribution
a. Racial geography of apartheid

What is the focus of the self-evaluation questions?
Select one:
a. Land Act of 1913
b. Post apartheid schooling
c. Missionary encounters
d. Decolonization of the curriculum
d. Decolonization of the curriculum

What is the focus of the self-evaluation questions?
Select one:
a. Land Act of 1913
b. Post apartheid schooling
c. Missionary encounters
d. Decolonization of the curriculum
d. Decolonization of the curriculum

What does FET stands for in the schooling system
Select one:
a. Further Education Training
b. Future Educational Training
c. school based curriculum for high school
d. First Education Teaching
a. Further Education Training

In the context of educational inequality,what does the term Global North-Global South divide refer to?
Select one:
a. The division between urban and rural areas in global education systems
b. The disparity in educational opportunities between northern and southern hemisphere countries
c. The divide between developed and developing countries in terms of education
d. The cultural and linguistic differences between countries in the northern and southern hemispheres
c. The divide between developed and developing countries in terms of education

Who proposed the concept of the "trinity of inequality" in education?
Select one:
a. Fairclough
b. Seroto, Davids and Wolhuter
c. Ashar
d. Van Dijk
b. Seroto, Davids and Wolhuter

What is the role of African missionaries and women in missionary work?
Select one:
a. Reinforcing racial segregation
b. Contributing to decolonization efforts
c. Encouraging cultural assimilation
d. Promoting colonial ideologies

The answer may vary depending on the specific historical context and individuals involved. However, in general, African missionaries and women in missionary work played various roles, including:

b. Contributing to decolonization efforts: Some African missionaries and women involved in missionary work played active roles in advocating for social justice, equality, and the liberation of their communities from colonial oppression.

c. Encouraging cultural assimilation: Missionary work often included efforts to convert indigenous peoples to Christianity and, in the process, promote Western cultural and religious norms. This could lead to the assimilation of some cultural practices and beliefs.

d. Promoting colonial ideologies: In some cases, missionary work aligned with colonial agendas and sought to reinforce colonial ideologies, including views of racial superiority and the superiority of Western civilization.

It is important to note that the role of African missionaries and women in missionary work was complex and multifaceted, with different individuals holding varying views and engaging in different activities.

What is the role of African missionaries and women in missionary work?
Select one:
a. Reinforcing racial segregation
b. Contributing to decolonization efforts
c. Encouraging cultural assimilation
d. Promoting colonial ideologies

The role of African missionaries and women in missionary work can be varied depending on the specific historical context and individuals involved. Some possible roles include:

b. Contributing to decolonization efforts: Some African missionaries and women involved in missionary work played significant roles in challenging colonial oppression, advocating for social justice, and supporting the liberation movement in their respective countries.

c. Encouraging cultural assimilation: Missionary work often involved efforts to spread Christianity and Western cultural norms, leading to the assimilation of some indigenous cultural practices and beliefs into the dominant culture promoted by the missionaries.

d. Promoting colonial ideologies: In certain instances, missionary work aligned with the colonial agenda, perpetuating and promoting colonial ideologies such as the superiority of Western civilization or racial segregation.

It is important to note that the roles of African missionaries and women in missionary work were not uniform, as individuals had different motivations, beliefs, and impacts on local communities.
